Alright, you're back. Now here's the deal before I go any further into the looping process. We're pretty far ready, right? But there are some important distinctions I want to clarify here when it comes to how you go about handling the actual objections themselves in terms of the rebuttals, right? Now, for starters, remember, and I've said this a few times now, at the end of the day, there really only between 12 or 14 objections, depending on how you categorize them and break them out. And also, depending on your industry, and half of them are pretty much the slight variations of two. But let me put a list up of you right here just to refresh your recollection. So let's go through them right now. There's, let me think about it. Let me call you back. Send me some information. I'm not liquid right now. I'm too busy. I have another supplier slash broke where I work with. I need to shop around. It's too expensive. Too much work. Sounds too good to be true. I need to do some research. My favorite of all the bedtime years, and it's Christmas time, tax time, back to school time, right? Groundhogs day. Need to speak to someone else as in my wife, my husband, my lawyer, my accountant, the tooth fairy, the butcher, the baker, you get it, right? And I don't do business over the phone. Those are your basic common objections. And I'm sure if you dug a lot of, you might be able to find one specific to your industry, but here's the deal. I'm going to go through them right now and show you why they really are all about certainties. Let's start at the top. Let me think about it, which really means I want to think about it so I can become more certain. You get it? Let me call you back. So I can have time to think about it, do some research, check around so I can make myself what? More certain. How about this one? Send me some information so I can independently confirm that what you're saying is indeed true, which will then make me more certain. It always comes down to the same thing. I'm not liquid right now, so since money's tight, I need to be even more certain than usual because I can't afford to be wrong about this one. Get it? I'm too busy right now, and since time is money, I can only spend time on things that I'm almost 100% certain are going to benefit me, right? I have another supplier slash broker I work with. So since I'm already at a very high level of certainty with them, what's the point of switching? Unless I'm even more certain with you. You get it? I need to shop around so I can find out if the certainty I'm feeling inside about your product is appropriate or maybe you just impulse me. Make sense? It's too expensive, which means I'm not that certain your value proposition actually stacks up. That's what expensive means. It's too much work because I'm not certain the benefits your product will outweigh all the energy you have to put in to actually get those benefits. Always comes back to certainty. Sounds too good to be true. Since you failed to connect all the dots, I'm not logically certain enough that the idea truly makes sense. You get it? How about this one? I need to do some more research so I can make myself more what? Certain. It's a bad time of year. It's in Christmas time, tax time, back to school time. So I'm not certain that the benefits of your product are awesome enough to make it worth stretching myself at this inopportune time. You get it? Always certainty. I need to speak to someone else, my husband, my wife, my lawyer, my accountant so I can what? Confirm that my certainty is actually warranted and also make myself more certain in the process. I don't do business over the phone because without looking you in the eye, I find it very difficult to become certain enough that you're telling me the truth. So I'm not that certain. You get it? This is what's really going on with all the so-called common objections. And by the way, out of all of them, you know which one I like to hear most or I respect most? Here it is. Ready? I care afford it or I'm not liquid right now. You know why? Because at least it might be real. In other words, if my prospect tells me that they can't afford it, I'll at least give them the benefit of the doubt and so far as that maybe it is true they made it through the intelligence gathering phase by accident or maybe they thought they could afford it when they heard the actual price though they realized they couldn't. Either way though, when I get hit with that objection first, I'm still going to respond with those exact same words. I'm going to deflect that objection. The only difference is I'm also going to apply additional tonalities of empathy and understanding. So it'll sound like this. I totally understand what you're saying Bill. But let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? You hear the difference? I'll say it again. So in the first case without I'm not liquid, it's a I hear what you're saying. Yes, we're in rapport. Right? I hear what you're saying versus I totally understand what you're saying Bill. See the second one by layering on these new tonalities on top of the existing ones, it shows Bill that I actually care about his financial situation. I'm not just there to make a buck, make a commission. And this will ensure that we stay in tight rapport, so I can continue the process of building more certainty. Now, just to be clear though, the fact that my tonality showed Bill that I understood his situation and empathize with him in terms of not being liquid right now, that does not mean I'm simply going to accept his supposed inability to pay at face value. Rather, the fact that I remained in tight rapport with him simply keeps me in the game so I can now go about showing him why he can in fact afford my product, either by offering some type of payment plan, maybe a financing option or even to a step down sale, if the product I'm selling allows for that. Now again, the key to remember here is that always on your initial closing attempt, you're fully expecting to be hit with one of these common objections. So you're in a monologue should be just as expected, a smoke stream for uncertainty. Right? I'm going to now roll up my sleeves and earn my paycheck. And again, it doesn't matter which common objection they hit you with, because whichever one they choose, you're always going to respond exactly the same way. I hear what you're saying, Bill, but does the idea make sense to you? Do you get it? Right? You've been down this road already. So that's the deal. Now, here's what I want you to do. I want you to go now and take a quiz. And after you're done with your quiz, you're going to see a whole bunch of different exercises about rebuttals and so forth, right? And next to that, well, maybe on top of that, well, very close to it, you're going to see your resource here, which is an extensive manual where you're going to find rebuttals for literally every common objection, sometimes upwards of 30 or 40 rebuttals for one objection. The key here is to take the ones that best apply to your industry. And remember, you have to be ethical here. Don't make up objections. Some will apply to some people, others to other people. That's why there's so many. I'm giving you so many options. Choose the ones that are ethical and that also have the appropriate context for the objection you hear in your industry, in your situation. And then go through the resource I gave you, the list of rebuttals, choose the right ones, be ethical, lock this stuff down. I promise you, you will be in an amazing spot. And with that, let's dive right back into actual looping. Now that you're on with rebuttals, I'll see you soon.
